goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Morning, Ameena. You said you're feeling great this morning with an 86 — that's high energy and a strong start.

Let's look at last night first: you got **9.3 hours** of sleep, which is way above your usual. That's a huge win for recovery time. Your HRV was strong at 112ms and you had good deep sleep, so your body got some solid repair work done. The catch? You were awake for 17% of that time, so it was a bit fragmented — maybe not as restful as it could have been.

Yesterday, your mood was **77** and stress was only **12**, which is fantastic. But here's the interesting thing: your focus score was 37, which is low compared to that high energy. It's like your brain wasn't fully tapping into the good vibes. Also, you barely moved yesterday — only 4,444 steps — while your body was in full recovery mode. That's a unusual combo: your system was ready for more, but you stayed still.

The big story today is about **channeling this high subjective energy into your cognitive work** while honoring your body's recovery. You've got the mood and low stress to support a focused day; it's about directing that fuel.

Your actions today are designed to bridge that gap — turning your great mood into tangible focus and maintaining the stability you've built.

With your stress this low and sleep that long, you have a real opportunity to make today both productive and grounded.

generated_actions.title: When you start your first work block, set a 45-minute timer and hide your phone
fallbacks: If 45 minutes feels too long, start with a 25-minute timer.
generated_actions.rationale: Your focus score (37) remains low despite high mood (77) and energy (76), with a focus-energy gap of -39. This structured, time-bound action directly tackles the 'monitor focus-energy mismatch' soft constraint by creating a distraction-free environment to channel your high subjective energy into cognitive work.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set a 45-minute timer and put your phone away for your first work block?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the timer and avoiding phone use for the duration, aiming to improve today's focus score from yesterday's 37.

assumptions: You have access to a timer (computer, watch, or separate device)
Your work does not critically require phone access for the next 45 minutes
generated_actions.description: Right as you begin your first focused work session, set a 45-minute countdown timer on your computer or a separate device. Place your smartphone face down or in another room until the timer goes off.

must_avoid: checking your phone during the work block

generated_actions.title: At 3 PM, stand up and do 10 slow shoulder rolls
fallbacks: If you miss 3 PM, do it at the next natural break.
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sedentary time was high (637 min) amid a 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'. This tiny, time-anchored action introduces a deliberate physical pause to disrupt prolonged sitting, promote bodily awareness, and support stability against the 'volatility' risk flag, leveraging your low stress (12) to make it easy to adopt.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you stand up and do 10 slow shoulder rolls at 3 PM?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the brief movement break at the specified time.

assumptions: You will be at a desk or seated environment in the afternoon
You can safely perform gentle shoulder movements
generated_actions.description: When your clock hits 3:00 PM this afternoon, stand up from your desk and perform 10 slow, deliberate shoulder rolls (5 forward, 5 backward). Focus on the movement and your breath.

generated_actions.title: At iftar tonight, add one extra spoon of a protein source to your plate
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's protein intake was low (35.1g) and on a steep downward trend. Your iftar description included beef kidney stew—this action builds on that by making a tiny, specific adjustment to gently boost protein within your Ramadan pattern, addressing the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag while respecting improved logging confidence (0.70).

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you add an extra spoon of protein to your iftar meal?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally increasing the protein portion of their evening meal.

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan and will have an iftar meal
Protein sources are available
generated_actions.description: When you break your fast this evening, consciously add one more spoonful of a protein-rich food (like the beef kidney stew, yogurt, or eggs) to your meal before you start eating.

generated_actions.title: After your evening prayer/ritual, dim the main lights
fallbacks: If you don't have dimmable lights, turn off some lights and use a smaller lamp.
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was long but inefficient (82.9%) with elevated wake time (17.1%). Habit-stacking light reduction onto an existing evening routine supports melatonin production and sleep onset, directly targeting sleep efficiency. This respects your '6am basketball practices midweek' constraint by promoting earlier, higher-quality wind-down.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you dim the main lights after your evening ritual?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports reducing ambient light intensity for at least 60 minutes before bed.

assumptions: You have control over your lighting environment
You engage in an evening prayer or winding-down routine
generated_actions.description: Immediately after completing your evening prayer or winding-down ritual tonight, turn off or dim the main overhead lights. Use only lamps, nightlights, or natural light for the next 60 minutes.

assumptions: User is observing Ramadan (meal types: suhoor, iftar)
User can access a smartphone for reminders
User can safely perform gentle stretches
User has a desk or seated work environment

evidence_used.goal_constraints: tight schedule, high workload, 6am basketball practices midweek

soft_constraints: avoid pushing recovery further given recent extreme sleep duration (9.3h)
ensure evening wind-down respects early morning constraint

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you feel afternoon energy dip, do 2 minutes of focused breathing
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's subjective energy was critically low (24) despite a high overall score (73), and the subjective-objective gap is large (+21) and volatile. This implementation intention provides a friction-reduction tool to address the 'low energy score' soft constraint and 'mismatch_pattern' risk flag by grounding you in the present moment when fatigue hits.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you pause for a 2-minute breathing reset when you felt an energy dip this afternoon?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally taking a brief breathing break in response to an energy dip.

assumptions: You can take a brief mental break when needed
previous_day_generated_actions.description: If you notice a drop in energy or focus this afternoon, stop for 2 minutes. Sit upright, close your eyes, and take 10 slow, deep breaths, focusing only on the sensation of air moving in and out.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a phone reminder for 10:30 PM to start winding down
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was exceptionally long with very early onset (43 min after midnight), a sharp reversal from recent late trends. However, your '6am basketball practices midweek' constraint conflicts with late sleep. This reminder anchors a consistent pre-bed routine to protect against schedule drift and supports today's 'sleep_schedule_consistency_with_recovery_focus' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set and honor the 10:30 PM wind-down reminder tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the reminder AND starting wind-down activities around that time, aiming for earlier sleep onset than recent extremes.

assumptions: You have a smartphone or device for reminders
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Set a recurring alarm or calendar reminder for 10:30 PM tonight titled 'Wind Down Start'. When it goes off, begin transitioning to low-light, low-stimulus activities (no new work, dim lights).

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Do a 3-minute gentle leg stretch right before getting into bed
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's activity was critically low (950 steps, 0 MVPA), which may contribute to physical stiffness despite long sleep. This low-effort movement promotes muscle relaxation and blood flow before bed, potentially improving sleep onset and efficiency, and addresses the 'low_recovery' risk flag by supporting physical recovery.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you do 3 minutes of gentle leg stretches immediately before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing brief, gentle leg stretching as part of their pre-sleep routine.

assumptions: You can safely perform gentle leg stretches
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you are about to get into bed tonight, spend 3 minutes doing slow, gentle hamstring and calf stretches while standing or sitting on the edge of the bed.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After your first work block, take 60 seconds to look out a window
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Given your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' constraints, along with yesterday's low focus (57) and critically low energy (24), this tiny habit-stacked action provides a micro-break to reset visual and mental fatigue. It supports stability by introducing a deliberate pause, reducing cognitive spillover, and addressing the 'volatility' risk flag.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a 60-second window gaze break after your first work block?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally pausing after a work session to look into the distance for about a minute.

assumptions: You have access to a window or outdoor view
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you finish your first concentrated work session today, immediately get up and spend 60 seconds looking out a window at the farthest point you can see, letting your eyes relax.

must_avoid: looking at screens during this time

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena. You're reporting a 73 today with high energy — that's a solid start.

First, about last night: you got a whopping 9.38 hours of sleep, which is way above your usual and you fell asleep much earlier than you have been. That's a big shift. But your body's recovery signals tell a different story — your HRV is declining and below your baseline, which means even with all that time in bed, your system didn't get its usual deep recharge. This mismatch is key. Yesterday, you felt really low energy (24) and mood (45), and you also moved very little — only 950 steps with zero intense activity. Your nutrition was also light at 878 calories, which during Ramadan can definitely contribute to that drained feeling. So the picture is: a long, early night of sleep, but your body is still playing catch-up from a day of low fuel and low movement.

The good news is your stress was low yesterday (28), which is a real strength to build on. It means you have some mental space to work with today. Given all this, today is about honoring that high-energy feeling you woke up with, while gently supporting your body's actual need for recovery and stability after a taxing day.

Your actions today focus on creating small, intentional pauses to help stabilize your energy and protect your sleep rhythm.

You've got the awareness — today's about using those early bedtimes and low-stress moments as your foundation.

evidence_quotes: I hadn't seen them for around 2 months
home workout which felt nice also and gave me some energy

meal_descriptions.description: 2 table spoons of fava beans, a fried egg and 2 table spoons on feta cheese with tomato and olive oil and one Arabic bread loaf
Large spoon of beef kidney stew and 3 spoons of rice and mulukhiyah and a handful of homemade fries
Instant coffee and a Turkish delight cube
